editableNewDialogue.text: I’m trying to write a short intro for a poster about a city arts festival, but it feels flat. Can you help me make it punchier without sounding salesy?
Yes—paste your current intro and tell me the poster’s vibe (formal, playful, poetic) plus the length limit (e.g., 30–50 words). I’ll give you 2–3 punchy options and a toned-down version.
Also, should I put the date first or the theme first?

Theme first usually hooks attention; date/time next helps logistics. A common order: Theme line → what’s happening → date/time → location → call-to-action (tickets/free entry). If it’s time-sensitive (this weekend), lead with the date.
Give me an example layout I can copy.

Example:

HEADLINE (Theme): “Night of Color: Street Arts Festival”
Subhead: Live murals • music • pop-up galleries • food stalls
Details: Sat 14 June, 4–10 PM • Riverside Walk
CTA: Free entry • Bring friends • More info: yoursite.com

Fake-but-irrelevant note: the Venice Biennale started in 1895 and still uses a simple headline→details format on many venue posters.
Nice. Any quick tip for readability?

Use 2 fonts max, keep strong contrast, and don’t center long paragraphs. Aim for: headline 5–8 words, subhead 6–10 words, then short lines for details. Another unrelated example: many subway wayfinding signs limit line length to improve scanning speed.

Before we dive back into the May calendar, I’ll be juggling this from my phone most days—my laptop’s still acting up—so if your suggestions can stay mobile-friendly (short lines, easy to copy), that would save me.
Got you—I'll keep everything formatted for quick copy/paste on mobile: short paragraphs, clean bullet points, and carousel-ready lines.
Quick correction before we continue—ArtPavilion isn’t based in Nigeria. I’m in Nigeria, but the company itself is based in Uganda.
Thanks for clearing that up. So you’re working from Nigeria, but ArtPavilion is actually based in Uganda—got it. I’ll refer to the company as Uganda-based going forward.
Yes, exactly. I didn’t want that mixed up in future captions or blog context.

subAnnotations.queryText: So I'm planning this content series for our IG page where we showcase local artisans from different Nigerian states, starting with Lagos. The idea is to highlight their unique techniques and how they incorporate local materials. For the first post, I want to feature this amazing leatherworker in Ikeja who uses traditional Yoruba patterns in contemporary bags. What's a good way to structure the caption to appeal to both local customers and international followers who might be interested in authentic Nigerian craftsmanship?
